question: [File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn / burnTorus] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the double-increment of totalTorusBurnt under the precondition that liquidityAdded==true and a non-zero titanXIntervalsBurn allocation exists, by calling swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn which first does totalTorusBurnt += torusAmount (line 246) and then calls burnTorus() which does totalTorusBurnt += torusToken.balanceOf(address(this)) (line 447) where balanceOf equals torusAmount, violating the invariant that totalTorusBurnt == sum of all Torus tokens actually destroyed, causing scoped impact: totalTorusBurnt accounting is inflated 2x per burn cycle, corrupting any downstream logic or off-chain accounting that relies on this value as a canonical burn counter? Local proof idea: fork-test at block 25530134, call swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn, assert totalTorusBurnt == torusToken.totalSupply_delta * 2 and compare against actual ERC20 burn events.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: swapETHForTorusAndBurn / burnTorus] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the same double-increment of totalTorusBurnt in swapETHForTorusAndBurn under the precondition that liquidityAdded==true and a non-zero ethIntervalsBurn allocation exists, by observing that line 286 does totalTorusBurnt += torusAmount and then line 293 calls burnTorus() which again does totalTorusBurnt += torusToken.balanceOf(address(this)) equaling torusAmount, violating the invariant totalTorusBurnt == actual cumulative Torus burned, causing scoped impact: same 2x inflation of totalTorusBurnt per ETH burn cycle, making the accounting variable permanently diverge from on-chain reality? Local proof idea: fork-test, call swapETHForTorusAndBurn, capture TorusBurned event amount, assert totalTorusBurnt == 2 * event_amount after one call.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: burnTorus] Can an unprivileged attacker call burnTorus() directly at any time under the precondition that the contract holds a non-zero Torus balance (e.g., after a partial build step or fee collection), burning protocol-held Torus that was earmarked for liquidity addition in _addLiquidityBuild, violating the invariant that Torus held by the contract between swap and liquidity-add steps is not externally destroyable, causing scoped impact: Torus intended for LP seeding is permanently destroyed, reducing protocol-owned liquidity depth and the value of the LP position? Local proof idea: fork-test, call swapETHForTorusAndBuild in a way that leaves Torus in the contract (simulate partial liquidity add), then call burnTorus() from a separate EOA and assert LP liquidity is less than expected.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: burnFees] Can an unprivileged attacker call burnFees() at any time under the precondition that the LP position (lpToken.tokenId) has accrued swap fees, causing all TitanX fees to be transferred to torusToken.genesisWallet() and all Torus fees to be burned, violating the invariant that fee distribution requires privileged authorization, causing scoped impact: accumulated LP fees are drained from the protocol's LP position to genesisWallet without any access control, bypassing any intended fee-accumulation period or governance decision? Local proof idea: fork-test, simulate swap activity to accrue fees, call burnFees() from an arbitrary EOA, assert TitanX balance of genesisWallet increased and Torus was burned.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: burnFees / burnTorus] Can an unprivileged attacker call burnFees() when lpToken.tokenId == 0 (before liquidityAdded is set) under the precondition that liquidityAdded==false, causing POSITION_MANAGER.collect() to be called with tokenId=0 which may collect fees from an unrelated position or return zero, and then burnTorus() is called on any Torus balance the contract holds, violating the invariant that fee collection only operates on the protocol's own LP position, causing scoped impact: Torus held by the contract for other purposes (e.g., pre-liquidity mint) is burned prematurely? Local proof idea: deploy fresh instance, call burnFees() before addLiquidityToTorusTitanXPool, assert no Torus is burned and no unintended position is touched.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: addLiquidityToTorusTitanXPool] Can an unprivileged attacker call addLiquidityToTorusTitanXPool() under the precondition that liquidityAdded==false and totalTitanXBurn >= INITIAL_TITAN_X_FOR_LIQ (200_000_000_000e18), triggering torusToken.mintTokensForLP() which mints INITIAL_LP_MINT (20_000e18) Torus to the contract, and then adding liquidity at a deadline the attacker controls, violating the invariant that initial liquidity seeding is a privileged or at least time-gated operation, causing scoped impact: attacker can front-run the legitimate liquidity add with a manipulated deadline or pool price, causing the initial LP to be seeded at an unfavorable price that benefits the attacker via immediate arbitrage? Local proof idea: fork-test pre-liquidity state, manipulate pool sqrtPrice, call addLiquidityToTorusTitanXPool with a past deadline to force revert or with a manipulated price to extract arbitrage.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: setCreateAndStakeContract] Can an unprivileged attacker call setCreateAndStakeContract(maliciousAddress) under the precondition that torusCreateAndStake == address(0) (i.e., before the constructor's setCreateAndStakeContract call completes or in a redeployment scenario), setting torusCreateAndStake to an attacker-controlled contract, violating the invariant that only the owner can configure critical system addresses, causing scoped impact: all subsequent calls that interact with torusCreateAndStake route through the attacker's contract, enabling arbitrary manipulation of the create-and-stake flow? Local proof idea: deploy TorusBuyAndProcess standalone (not via Torus constructor), immediately call setCreateAndStakeContract from a non-owner EOA, assert it succeeds.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: releaseFractalFunds] Can an unprivileged attacker call releaseFractalFunds() at any time after block.timestamp >= lastFractalRelease + FRACTAL_RELEASE_INTERVAL (110 days) under the precondition that fractalTitanX > 0 or fractalETH > 0, moving all fractal reserves into totalTitanXBurn and totalETHBurn immediately, violating the invariant that fractal fund release timing is controlled by the protocol, causing scoped impact: attacker can trigger premature release to inflate the current interval's allocation, then immediately call swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn or swapETHForTorusAndBurn to extract a larger incentive than intended? Local proof idea: fork-test, warp to lastFractalRelease + 110 days + 1, call releaseFractalFunds() then immediately call swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn, compare incentive received vs expected without fractal release.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn / _swapTitanXForTorus / getTorusQuoteForTitanX] Can an unprivileged attacker manipulate the Uniswap V3 TWAP oracle for the TitanX/TORUS pool under the precondition that the pool has low liquidity and the attacker can move the price for 15 minutes (or less if oldestObservation < 900s), causing getTorusQuoteForTitanX to return a deflated amountOutMinimum, then calling swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn to execute a swap at a price far below fair value, violating the invariant that protocol swaps execute at or near market price, causing scoped impact: protocol TitanX is swapped for far fewer Torus tokens than fair value, with the attacker capturing the price difference via a sandwich or pre-positioned trade? Local proof idea: fork-test with a flash-loan to move pool price, warp 15 minutes, call swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn, measure Torus received vs fair-value Torus, assert attacker profit > 0.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: swapETHForTorusAndBurn / _swapETHForTitanX / getTitanXQuoteForETH] Can an unprivileged attacker manipulate the Uniswap V3 TWAP oracle for the TITAN_X_ETH_POOL (0xc45a81bc23a64ea556ab4cdf08a86b61cdceea8b) under the precondition that the attacker can move the ETH/TitanX price for the TWAP window, causing getTitanXQuoteForETH to return a deflated amountOutMinimum for the ETH→TitanX swap, then calling swapETHForTorusAndBurn to execute at a manipulated price, violating the invariant that protocol ETH is converted to TitanX at fair market rates, causing scoped impact: protocol ETH (from the live 3.42 ETH balance) is drained at below-market rates with the attacker capturing the spread? Local proof idea: fork-test, manipulate TITAN_X_ETH_POOL TWAP, call swapETHForTorusAndBurn, assert ETH spent > fair-value TitanX received * market_price.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: swapETHForTorusAndBuild / _swapETHForTitanX / _addLiquidityBuild] Can an unprivileged attacker sandwich the swapETHForTorusAndBuild call under the precondition that liquidityAddedBuild==true and a non-zero ethIntervalsBuild allocation exists, by front-running to move the ETH→TitanX pool price, causing _swapETHForTitanX to receive fewer TitanX, then _addLiquidityBuild to have leftover tokens sent to genesisWallet (lines 1010-1014), violating the invariant that protocol ETH converts to LP liquidity at fair value, causing scoped impact: protocol ETH is partially drained to genesisWallet via slippage-induced leftovers rather than being added to the LP position? Local proof idea: fork-test, front-run swapETHForTorusAndBuild with a large pool price move, assert genesisWallet received non-trivial TitanX/Torus from leftover transfers.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: swapTitanXForTorusAndBuild / _addLiquidityBuild] Can an unprivileged attacker sandwich the swapTitanXForTorusAndBuild call under the precondition that liquidityAddedBuild==true and a non-zero titanXIntervalsBuild allocation exists, by manipulating the TitanX/TORUS pool price before the call, causing _swapTitanXForTorus to receive fewer Torus, then _addLiquidityBuild to send the unused Torus/TitanX remainder to genesisWallet, violating the invariant that all allocated TitanX is converted to LP liquidity, causing scoped impact: protocol TitanX is partially drained to genesisWallet via slippage-induced leftover transfers in _addLiquidityBuild? Local proof idea: fork-test, manipulate TitanX/TORUS pool price, call swapTitanXForTorusAndBuild, assert genesisWallet balance increased by more than dust.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: swapETHForTorusAndBurn] Can an unprivileged attacker observe that currInterval.amountBurned is written twice (line 270 and line 275) in swapETHForTorusAndBurn under the precondition that the function is called normally, and determine whether the duplicate write creates any window where the interval guard (amountBurned != 0 check at line 268) could be bypassed in a future call, violating the invariant that each ETH burn interval is executed exactly once, causing scoped impact: if the duplicate write creates a state inconsistency, an attacker could replay the same interval to extract double the ETH incentive? Local proof idea: unit-test, call swapETHForTorusAndBurn twice in the same interval, assert the second call reverts with IntervalAlreadyBurned.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: _calculateIntervalsTitanXBurn / swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the missed-interval accumulation in _calculateIntervalsTitanXBurn under the precondition that many intervals have been skipped (e.g., no one called swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn for N * INTERVAL_TIME seconds), causing _totalAmountForInterval = amountPerInterval * (1 + missedIntervals) to approach totalTitanXBurn, then calling swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn to extract the maximum possible incentive (INCENTIVE_FEE=150 bps of the full allocation), violating the invariant that per-interval incentive is bounded by DAILY_ALLOCATION_TITAN_X_BURNING / INTERVALS_PER_DAY of the daily pool, causing scoped impact: attacker extracts a disproportionately large TitanX incentive by waiting for many missed intervals to accumulate? Local proof idea: fork-test, warp forward 30 days without calling the burn function, call swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn, measure incentive received vs expected single-interval incentive.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: _calculateIntervalsETHBurn / swapETHForTorusAndBurn] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the missed-interval accumulation in _calculateIntervalsETHBurn under the precondition that many ETH burn intervals have been skipped, causing _totalAmountForInterval to be capped at totalETHBurn (the full ETH burn pool), then calling swapETHForTorusAndBurn to extract INCENTIVE_FEE (150 bps) of the entire totalETHBurn as an ETH incentive, violating the invariant that per-call ETH incentive is bounded by a small fraction of the daily allocation, causing scoped impact: attacker extracts up to 1.5% of the entire ETH burn pool (currently ~99 ETH worth) as a single incentive payment? Local proof idea: fork-test, warp 30+ days, call swapETHForTorusAndBurn, assert incentive received == 150 * totalETHBurn / 10000.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: _calculateIntervalsTitanXBuild / swapTitanXForTorusAndBuild] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit missed-interval accumulation in _calculateIntervalsTitanXBuild under the precondition that many build intervals have been skipped, causing _totalAmountForInterval to be capped at totalTitanXForBuild, then calling swapTitanXForTorusAndBuild to extract INCENTIVE_FEE_BUILD (150 bps) of the entire totalTitanXForBuild as a TitanX incentive, violating the invariant that per-call build incentive is bounded by a small fraction of the daily allocation, causing scoped impact: attacker extracts up to 1.5% of the entire TitanX build pool as a single incentive payment? Local proof idea: fork-test, warp 30+ days without build calls, call swapTitanXForTorusAndBuild, assert incentive == 150 * totalTitanXForBuild / 10000.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: _calculateIntervalsETHBuild / swapETHForTorusAndBuild] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit missed-interval accumulation in _calculateIntervalsETHBuild under the precondition that many ETH build intervals have been skipped, causing _totalAmountForInterval to be capped at totalETHForBuild, then calling swapETHForTorusAndBuild to extract INCENTIVE_FEE_BUILD (150 bps) of the entire totalETHForBuild as an ETH incentive, violating the invariant that per-call ETH build incentive is bounded by a small fraction of the daily allocation, causing scoped impact: attacker extracts up to 1.5% of the entire ETH build pool as a single ETH incentive payment? Local proof idea: fork-test, warp 30+ days without build calls, call swapETHForTorusAndBuild, assert ETH incentive received == 150 * totalETHForBuild / 10000.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: distributeETHForBurning / distributeETHForBuilding / swapETHForTorusAndBurn / swapETHForTorusAndBuild] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the shared ETH balance between totalETHBurn and totalETHForBuild under the precondition that both pools have non-zero allocations and the contract's actual ETH balance is less than totalETHBurn + totalETHForBuild + fractalETH, by calling swapETHForTorusAndBurn to drain ETH from the shared balance, causing swapETHForTorusAndBuild to revert when it tries to send the ETH incentive, violating the invariant that both burn and build ETH pools are independently solvent, causing scoped impact: one flow can starve the other of ETH, causing permanent DoS of the build or burn incentive payment? Local proof idea: fork-test, drain totalETHBurn to near-zero via repeated burn calls, then call swapETHForTorusAndBuild and assert it reverts on the incentive transfer.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: swapETHForTorusAndBurn] Can an unprivileged attacker deploy a contract that reverts or consumes more than 30000 gas in its receive() function, then call swapETHForTorusAndBurn from that contract under the precondition that a non-zero ETH burn interval exists, causing the ETH incentive transfer (line 294-298) to fail and the entire transaction to revert with 'Transfer failed', violating the invariant that any EOA or contract can trigger the burn and receive the incentive, causing scoped impact: the ETH burn interval is permanently locked (amountBurned is already set at line 270 before the revert), preventing any future caller from executing that interval? Local proof idea: deploy a contract with a gas-consuming receive(), call swapETHForTorusAndBurn from it, assert the interval's amountBurned is set but the transaction reverts, then verify no other caller can execute that interval.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: swapETHForTorusAndBurn] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that currInterval.amountBurned is written at line 270 BEFORE the ETH incentive transfer at line 294, under the precondition that the incentive transfer fails (e.g., caller is a contract with a reverting receive()), causing the transaction to revert but leaving amountBurned already written in storage (since Solidity 0.8 reverts all state changes on revert), and then determine whether the interval is permanently locked or can be replayed, violating the invariant that a failed burn attempt does not consume the interval allocation, causing scoped impact: if the interval is locked, the ETH allocated to that interval is permanently stuck in totalETHBurn? Local proof idea: unit-test with a reverting receiver, call swapETHForTorusAndBurn, assert the interval amountBurned is 0 after revert (Solidity reverts all state), then call again from an EOA and assert it succeeds.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: releaseFractalFunds / distributeTitanXForBurning] Can an unprivileged attacker call distributeTitanXForBurning with a small amount to trigger releaseFractalFunds() (via the inline check at line 521-523) under the precondition that block.timestamp >= lastFractalRelease + FRACTAL_RELEASE_INTERVAL, causing fractalTitanX to be moved to totalTitanXBurn and fractalETH to be moved to totalETHBurn, then immediately calling swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn to capture the inflated interval allocation, violating the invariant that fractal release and burn execution are independent operations, causing scoped impact: attacker atomically releases fractal funds and captures the resulting larger incentive in a single transaction? Local proof idea: fork-test, warp to fractal release time, call distributeTitanXForBurning(1) then swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn in the same block, compare incentive vs without fractal release.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: distributeETHForBuilding / releaseFractalFunds] Can an unprivileged attacker call distributeETHForBuilding{value: 1 wei}() to trigger releaseFractalFunds() (via the inline check at line 890-892) under the precondition that block.timestamp >= lastFractalRelease + FRACTAL_RELEASE_INTERVAL, causing fractalETH to be moved to totalETHBurn (not totalETHForBuild), then immediately calling swapETHForTorusAndBurn to capture the inflated ETH burn allocation, violating the invariant that ETH deposited for building stays in the build pool, causing scoped impact: attacker uses a 1-wei build deposit to trigger fractal release and then extracts a larger ETH burn incentive? Local proof idea: fork-test, warp to fractal release time, call distributeETHForBuilding{value:1}() then swapETHForTorusAndBurn, assert ETH incentive is larger than without fractal release.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn / totalTitanXBurn accounting] Can an unprivileged attacker observe that totalTitanXBurn is decremented by currInterval.amountAllocated (line 247) but the actual TitanX swapped is titanXToSwapAndBurn = amountAllocated - incentive, under the precondition that INCENTIVE_FEE=150 bps, causing totalTitanXBurn to decrease by more than the TitanX actually consumed in the swap, violating the invariant that totalTitanXBurn tracks the TitanX available for burning (not including incentive payments), causing scoped impact: totalTitanXBurn is systematically understated by the incentive amount per call, causing future interval allocations to be calculated on a smaller base than the actual TitanX held? Local proof idea: invariant fuzz test, after N calls to swapTitanXForTorusAndBurn, assert titanX.balanceOf(contract) >= totalTitanXBurn + fractalTitanX.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: swapETHForTorusAndBurn / totalETHBurn accounting] Can an unprivileged attacker observe that totalETHBurn is decremented by currInterval.amountAllocated (line 287) which includes the incentive, but the ETH incentive is paid from the contract's native balance, under the precondition that totalETHBurn tracks the ETH available for burning, causing totalETHBurn to decrease by amountAllocated while only ethToSwapAndBurn ETH is used for the swap and incentive ETH is paid separately, violating the invariant that address(this).balance >= totalETHBurn + totalETHForBuild + fractalETH at all times, causing scoped impact: if the ETH balance accounting diverges, future swap calls may revert due to insufficient ETH? Local proof idea: invariant fuzz test, after N calls to swapETHForTorusAndBurn, assert address(this).balance >= totalETHBurn + totalETHForBuild + fractalETH.

[File: 'src/contracts/src/TorusBuyAndProcess.sol -> Scope: Critical fund extraction or protocol value drain by an unprivileged attacker'] [Function: distributeETHForBurning / distributeETHForBuilding] Can an unprivileged attacker observe the asymmetry that distributeETHForBurning splits ETH into fractalETH (28%) and totalETHBurn (72%), while distributeETHForBuilding puts 100% into totalETHForBuild with no fractal split, under the precondition that both functions are callable by anyone, causing the build pool to accumulate ETH faster than the burn pool relative to deposits, violating the invariant that both pools have symmetric fractal-reserve mechanics, causing scoped impact: the build pool's totalETHForBuild grows without any fractal reserve, meaning all build ETH is immediately available for interval allocation and incentive extraction, allowing faster incentive drain from the build pool? Local proof idea: unit-test, deposit equal ETH to both functions, compare totalETHForBuild vs totalETHBurn + fractalETH, assert build pool has 28% more immediately available.
